text: Chapter 11 introduces jazz harmonic devices through analysis of Stella by Starlight, emphasizing that Real Book chord changes—not the original film version—form the standard for group performance. The chapter's analytical foundation uses color-coding to map secondary tonal centers back to a parent key, with the song's defining characteristic being a series of unresolved ii-V progressions in the opening that evade rather than establish a key center. Key concepts include the backdoor dominant (a bVII7 substituting for V7 by whole-step approach) and its extended form, the backdoor ii-V, which allow multiple harmonic interpretations of single chords; the hybrid ii-V-I, which mixes minor ii-V from a minor key while resolving to a major chord, creating an expressive surprise; and a closing cycle-of-4ths built from minor ii-Vs rather than dominant ii-Vs. The chapter prescribes practical improvisation tools: note-mapping to conceptualize available pitch choices, quarter-note solo exercises to isolate harmonic navigation from rhythm, and the dual-interpretation mindset that reads a single chord through multiple functional lenses simultaneously.
